subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: 2nd pass (2026-05-28, bias-corrected): Canadian + Francophone (Le Devoir, Radio-Canada) + IMF/OECD confirm and soften the catastrophist US framing (contained tariffs, real EU/SAFE realignment, improving deficit). Note: the budget survived confidence only 170-168, so the governing majority is thin. No score change.

Live-data refresh (2026-05-28): factors re-validated against diverse, independent sources (multilateral bodies + non-US/non-Anglophone press; state-self-reported data still excluded). Notes mark what moved.
v2.0 source-discipline re-run applied; Statistics Canada is independent and uncompromised, so no entanglement discount was needed.

Trade re-weighted 0.05 -> 0.17: Canada is an involuntary target of US tariff actions (near-term hostility) and is diversifying toward the EU/Asia (long-term adaptation). Resource-rich but strong institutions mean NO paradox-of-plenty curse - the resource base is an asset, not a trap.
Slant-balance audit: balanced; the main negatives (alliance targeting by the US, involuntary trade exposure) are externally observable.

economic.notes: A sound starting fiscal position (lowest debt burden in the G7); strong central-bank and banking institutions; the main drag is involuntary exposure to volatile US tariff actions, now weighted more heavily.

fiscal_state.notes: Deficit ~2.1% GDP declining to ~1.4% by 2030-31; lowest debt-to-GDP in the G7; Carney investment-led growth with planned operational cuts.

monetary_independence.notes: Bank of Canada strong independence; no political pressure campaign; inflation target intact.

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: CAD not a reserve currency; effectively neutral.

inflation.notes: Near the 2% target; less oil-shock exposure (own production); housing cost a structural problem.

banking_stability.notes: Big Six oligopoly, strong capital, conservative lending, robust OSFI.

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Feb 2026 US Supreme Court struck down the IEEPA tariffs; the US replaced them with a 10% Section 122 surcharge (USMCA-exempt) + retained auto/steel/aluminum tariffs - less hostile than the early-2026 peak but persistent, and Canada was left out of the first USMCA review round (leverage warning). Diversification now concrete (EU CETA, ASEAN). Near -5 -> -4; long -2 hold.

institutional.notes: Strong across the board; among the top 15 globally on rule of law.

rule_of_law.notes: Independent, respected Supreme Court; notwithstanding-clause friction operates within the rule-of-law framework; WJP top 15.

statistical_integrity.notes: Statistics Canada highly regarded; no documented interference.

civil_service_capacity.notes: Major restructuring; Carney planned 7.5-15% operational cuts on normal timelines.

press_freedom.notes: Consistently high rankings; CBC under pressure but operating; some consolidation concerns.

political_social.notes: Low polarization by international standards; immigration less welcoming than five years ago but orderly and within the rule of law.

political_stability.notes: Carney Liberal minority; functional and peaceful transfers the norm, BUT the spring budget survived a confidence vote only 170-168 (NDP/Green abstentions) - the majority is thin. Held at +3; the thinness is a watch item, not yet instability (CBC, Radio-Canada).

civil_liberties.notes: Charter protections; some concerns (Online Streaming Act, C-63, 2022 Emergencies Act use) within judicial oversight.

treatment_non_citizens.notes: 2026-28 plan holds PR steady at 380k/yr (not cut further); temporary residents cut hard (-45%), student visas down; all via published plan, due process, no mass deportation. +2 confirmed; confidence up (IRCC).

social_cohesion.notes: Rising polarization from a higher baseline; Quebec and Indigenous tensions managed; limited political violence.

demographics.notes: Growth was immigration-driven, now declining due to cuts; below-replacement native fertility; aging.

geopolitical.notes: Strong on conflict-avoidance and sanctions safety; negative only on alliance reliability due to direct US targeting.

alliance_reliability.notes: Five Eyes expulsion stayed RHETORIC, not action (Canada took part in the Nov 2025 Five Eyes summit); annexation rhetoric persists. Real action is the counter-pivot: Canada joined the EU SAFE EUR150B program (first non-European member) and deepened EU defence ties. Scored on actions -> -3 to -2.

conflict_involvement.notes: No active conflicts; limited NATO obligations; Arctic contested but not militarized; no Iran involvement.

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Follows Western sanctions; not a major imposer; no capital controls; some secondary exposure to US actions.

physical_practical.notes: Universal healthcare with access problems but strong aggregate outcomes; very low violent crime.

climate.notes: A net beneficiary near/mid-term (high-latitude agriculture plus Arctic access, very high readiness), but intensifying wildfire and permafrost thaw erode the gain in longer outlooks. (Burke et al.; ND-GAIN)

healthcare.notes: Universal single-payer; primary-care shortages and wait times; strong outcomes at moderate cost.

infrastructure.notes: Adequate; housing infrastructure investment planned; good transit in major cities; strong broadband.

crime_safety.notes: Homicide ~1/3 of the US; firearm violence vastly lower; mass shootings rare.

disaster_insurance.notes: Rising wildfire/flood in regions but insurance markets functional; federal response intact.

summary: Strong institutions across the board - independent courts, a respected statistical agency, high press freedom - a positive political/social state, and a sound fiscal position with the lowest debt burden in the G7. The main weaknesses are involuntary exposure to volatile US tariff actions and a degraded alliance position from being directly targeted, now partly offset by a concrete pivot to EU/Nordic frameworks. Resource wealth paired with strong institutions is a managed asset, not a curse.
